text.title: Feature Flag Cleanup Discipline Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the Feature Flag Cleanup Discipline methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: Every flag declared MUST carry an expiry date written when the flag is added. Flags without expiry are rejected at PR review.
rule.rationale: without expiry, the flag becomes a permanent fixture.

rule.statement: Every flag carries a single named owner. The owner is responsible for the cleanup PR.
If expiry is reached and flag is still on, owner has a 14-day grace to either re-state expiry (with reason) or ship the cleanup PR. Indefinite extensions are rejected.
rule.rationale: Grace prevents panic-deletes but caps the slip.

rule.statement: Cleanup PR removes BOTH branches (the old code path AND the flag declaration); leaving the disabled branch behind is rejected.
rule.rationale: Half-removed flags are worse than the original debt.

rule.statement: Cleanup PR MUST attach evidence the disabled path is gone (grep / coverage report). Self-asserted removal is rejected.
rule.rationale: removed flags have re-appeared in the codebase before.

rule.statement: Cleanup PR attaches a final flag-evaluation metric snapshot (last 7d hit count) so traffic shape at removal is documented.
rule.rationale: removing a high-traffic flag without a metric snapshot is unverifiable.

rule.statement: When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running Feature Flag Cleanup Discipline under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
